title: Polar Equations of Conics
objectives: Write polar equations of conics using the focus-directrix definition
Identify the type of conic from its eccentricity ($e<1$, $e=1$, $e>1$)
Convert between rectangular and polar forms of conics
Graph conics from their polar equations
Apply polar equations to planetary orbits and satellite paths

devotional.title: The Heavens Declare
devotional.content:
When Johannes Kepler discovered that planets travel in ellipses with the 
Sun at one focus, he wept. Not from frustration—from wonder. After years 
of trying to force the planets into perfect circles, he finally saw the 
truth: the orbits were ellipses, governed by three elegant laws that united 
every planet, moon, and comet into one mathematical symphony.

Kepler wrote: "I was merely thinking God's thoughts after Him. Since we 
astronomers are priests of the highest God in regard to the book of nature, 
it befits us to be thoughtful, not of the glory of our minds, but rather, 
above all else, of the glory of God."

What strikes us in this section is how a *single equation*—the polar 
form of a conic—describes the orbit of Mercury, the path of Halley's comet, 
and the trajectory of a spacecraft to Mars. The eccentricity $e$ is the only 
parameter that distinguishes a nearly circular planetary orbit from a 
wildly elongated comet's path. This economy of expression, this unity beneath 
diversity, bears the fingerprints of design.

*The heavens declare the glory of God; the skies proclaim the work of 
his hands. Day after day they pour forth speech; night after night they 
reveal knowledge.* (Psalm 19:1–2)

Every time we solve for a comet's perihelion or trace an orbit's polar 
equation, we participate in what Kepler called reading "the book of 
nature"—a book whose Author delights to be discovered.

content.text:
In Chapter 8, we introduced polar coordinates as an alternative way to
locate points in the plane. In this section, we discover that conic sections
have remarkably elegant equations when one focus is placed at the pole.
This polar approach unifies all three conics—parabola, ellipse, and
hyperbola—into a single family of equations distinguished only by
their eccentricity.
The Focus-Directrix Definition

Recall that we defined each conic geometrically: a parabola as the set
of points equidistant from a focus and directrix, an ellipse as the set
of points with constant sum of distances to two foci, and a hyperbola
as the set with constant difference. There is an alternative definition
that unifies all three.

content.title: Conic Section---Focus-Directrix Definition
content.content:
A **conic section** is the set of all points $P$ in the plane such
that the ratio of the distance from $P$ to a fixed point (the **focus**)
to the distance from $P$ to a fixed line (the **directrix**) is a
positive constant $e$, called the **eccentricity**:
$$
\frac{PF}{PD} = e
$$
where $PF$ is the distance from $P$ to the focus $F$, and $PD$ is the
perpendicular distance from $P$ to the directrix.

content.text: The value of $e$ determines which conic we obtain:

items: If $0 < e < 1$, the conic is an **ellipse**.

content.caption:
The focus-directrix definition of conics. For each conic, $P$ is a
point on the curve, $F$ is the focus, and the dashed line is the directrix.
The ratio $PF/PD$ equals the eccentricity $e$. When $e < 1$, points stay
closer to the focus than the directrix (ellipse). When $e = 1$, distances
are equal (parabola). When $e > 1$, points are farther from the directrix
than the focus (hyperbola).
content.alt:
The focus-directrix definition of conics. For each conic, $P$ is a
point on the curve, $F$ is the focus, and the dashed line is the directrix.
The ratio $PF/PD$ equals the eccentricity $e$. When $e < 1$, points stay
closer to the focus than the directrix (ellipse). When $e = 1$, distances
are equal (parabola). When $e > 1$, points are farther from the directrix
than the focus (hyperbola).

content.text:
For the parabola, this is exactly the definition we gave in Section 10.1
(since $e = 1$ means $PF = PD$). For the ellipse and hyperbola, this
definition is equivalent to our earlier ones, though the connection
requires some algebra to verify.
Deriving the Polar Equation

Place the focus at the pole (origin) and the directrix perpendicular to
the polar axis. Let $p$ be the distance from the focus to the directrix.

Consider a point $P = (r, \theta)$ on the conic. The distance from $P$ to
the focus is simply $r$. If the directrix is a vertical line to the right
of the pole at distance $p$, then the perpendicular distance from $P$ to
the directrix is $p - r\cos\theta$.

content.caption:
Deriving the polar equation. The focus $F$ is at the pole, and the
directrix is the vertical line $x = p$ at distance $p$ from the pole. For point
$P = (r, \theta)$, the distance to the focus is $r$, and the perpendicular
distance to the directrix is $p - r\cos\theta$.
content.alt:
Deriving the polar equation. The focus $F$ is at the pole, and the
directrix is the vertical line $x = p$ at distance $p$ from the pole. For point
$P = (r, \theta)$, the distance to the focus is $r$, and the perpendicular
distance to the directrix is $p - r\cos\theta$.

content.text:
By the focus-directrix definition:
$$
\frac{r}{p - r\cos\theta} = e
$$
Solving for $r$:

$$\begin{aligned}r &= e(p - r\cos\theta) \\
r &= ep - er\cos\theta \\
r + er\cos\theta &= ep \\
r(1 + e\cos\theta) &= ep\end{aligned}$$

content.title: Polar Equations of Conics
content.content:
The graph of a polar equation of the form
$$
r = \frac{ep}{1 \pm e\cos\theta} \quad \text{or} \quad r = \frac{ep}{1 \pm e\sin\theta}
$$
is a conic section, where $e > 0$ is the eccentricity and $p > 0$ is the
distance from the focus (at the pole) to the directrix.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
The choice of $\pm$ and $\cos\theta$ versus $\sin\theta$ determines the
orientation:

content.title: Identifying a Conic from Its Polar Equation
content.problem:
Identify the conic given by each equation and describe its features.

**(a)** $\displaystyle r = \frac{15}{3 - 2\cos\theta}$

**(b)** $\displaystyle r = \frac{6}{2 + 2\sin\theta}$

**(c)** $\displaystyle r = \frac{12}{2 - 3\cos\theta}$
content.solution:
To identify the conic, we first convert each equation to standard form
by dividing numerator and denominator so the constant term in the
denominator is 1.

**(a)** Divide numerator and denominator by 3:
$$
r = \frac{15}{3 - 2\cos\theta} = \frac{5}{1 - \frac{2}{3}\cos\theta}
$$
Here $e = \frac{2}{3} < 1$, so this is an **ellipse**. The form
$1 - e\cos\theta$ indicates the directrix is to the left of the pole.
Since $ep = 5$ and $e = \frac{2}{3}$, we have $p = \frac{15}{2}$.

**(b)** Divide by 2:
$$
r = \frac{6}{2 + 2\sin\theta} = \frac{3}{1 + \sin\theta}
$$
Here $e = 1$, so this is a **parabola**. The form $1 + e\sin\theta$
indicates the directrix is above the pole at $y = 3$.

**(c)** Divide by 2:
$$
r = \frac{12}{2 - 3\cos\theta} = \frac{6}{1 - \frac{3}{2}\cos\theta}
$$
Here $e = \frac{3}{2} > 1$, so this is a **hyperbola**. The directrix
is to the left of the pole.

content.text: Graphing Conics in Polar Form

To sketch a conic given in polar form, find key points by evaluating
at strategic angles: $\theta = 0, \pi/2, \pi, 3\pi/2$.

content.title: Graphing a Polar Ellipse
content.solution:
Divide by 2 to get standard form:
$$
r = \frac{3}{1 + \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta}
$$
Since $e = \frac{1}{2} < 1$, this is an ellipse.

Evaluate at key angles:

| $\theta$ | $0$ | $\pi/2$ | $\pi$ | $3\pi/2$ |
| $r$ | $\frac{6}{2+1} = 2$ | $\frac{6}{2+0} = 3$ | $\frac{6}{2-1} = 6$ | $3$ |

The vertices are at $(2, 0)$ and $(6, \pi)$. The major axis has length
$2a = 2 + 6 = 8$, so $a = 4$. The center is at distance $a - 2 = 2$ from
the pole along the polar axis (in the direction of $\theta = \pi$).

figure[H]

The polar ellipse $r = 6/(2 + \cos\theta)$ with $e = 1/2$. The focus
is at the pole (origin), vertices at $(2, 0)$ and $(6, \pi)$. The ellipse is
elongated along the polar axis with center at $(-2, 0)$.

figure

content.title: Graphing a Polar Parabola
content.solution:
This is already in standard form with $e = 1$, so it's a parabola.
The form $1 - \sin\theta$ indicates the directrix is below the pole.

Evaluate at key angles:

| $\theta$ | $0$ | $\pi/2$ | $\pi$ |
| $r$ | $\frac{4}{1-0} = 4$ | $\frac{4}{1-1} = \text{undefined}$ | $4$ |

At $\theta = 3\pi/2$: $r = \frac{4}{1-(-1)} = \frac{4}{2} = 2$ (this is the vertex).

The parabola has vertex at $(2, 3\pi/2)$ (i.e., at $(0, -2)$ in rectangular
coordinates), opens upward, and approaches infinity as $\theta \to \pi/2$.

figure[H]

The polar parabola $r = 4/(1 - \sin\theta)$ with $e = 1$. 
The focus is at the pole, the vertex is at $(0, -2)$, and the 
directrix is $y = -4$. The parabola opens upward.

figure

content.title: Graphing a Polar Hyperbola
content.solution:
Divide by 2: $r = \frac{9/2}{1 + \frac{3}{2}\cos\theta}$.

Since $e = \frac{3}{2} > 1$, this is a hyperbola. The directrix is
to the right of the pole.

Evaluate at key angles:

| $\theta$ | $0$ | $\pi/2$ | $\pi$ | $3\pi/2$ |
| $r$ | $\frac{9}{2+3} = \frac{9}{5}$ | $\frac{9}{2} = 4.5$ | $\frac{9}{2-3} = -9$ | $4.5$ |

The negative value at $\theta = \pi$ indicates that point is actually at
$(9, 0)$ (plotting a negative $r$ means going in the opposite direction).

The vertices of the hyperbola are at $(\frac{9}{5}, 0)$ and $(9, 0)$.

figure[H]

The polar hyperbola $r = 9/(2 + 3\cos\theta)$ with $e = 3/2$. 
The focus is at the pole. Both branches are shown: the right branch 
has vertex at $(9/5, 0)$, the left passes through $(9, 0)$. 
Dashed lines show the asymptotes.

figure

content.text: Finding Polar Equations

Given information about a conic's eccentricity and directrix, we can
write its polar equation directly.

content.title: Finding a Polar Equation
content.problem:
Find a polar equation for each conic with focus at the pole.

**(a)** A parabola with directrix $y = 3$

**(b)** An ellipse with eccentricity $\frac{1}{2}$ and directrix $x = -4$

**(c)** An ellipse with vertices at $(2, \pi/2)$ and $(6, 3\pi/2)$
content.solution:
**(a)** For a parabola, $e = 1$. The directrix $y = 3$ is a horizontal
line above the pole, so we use the form $1 + \sin\theta$. The distance
$p = 3$, giving:
$$
r = \frac{(1)(3)}{1 + \sin\theta} = \frac{3}{1 + \sin\theta}
$$

**(b)** We have $e = \frac{1}{2}$. The directrix $x = -4$ is a vertical
line to the left of the pole, so we use $1 - \cos\theta$. Here $p = 4$:
$$
r = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot 4}{1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta} = \frac{2}{1 - \frac{1}{2}\cos\theta}
$$
or equivalently: $r = \frac{4}{2 - \cos\theta}$.

**(c)** The vertices are on the line $\theta = \pi/2$, indicating
the major axis is vertical. Use the form $1 + e\sin\theta$.

At vertex $(2, \pi/2)$: $r = \frac{ep}{1 + e\sin(\pi/2)} = \frac{ep}{1+e} = 2$

At vertex $(6, 3\pi/2)$: $r = \frac{ep}{1 + e\sin(3\pi/2)} = \frac{ep}{1-e} = 6$

From these: $ep = 2(1+e)$ and $ep = 6(1-e)$.

Setting them equal: $2(1+e) = 6(1-e)$, so $2 + 2e = 6 - 6e$, giving $8e = 4$,
hence $e = \frac{1}{2}$.

Since $e < 1$, this confirms we have an ellipse. Now find $p$:
from $ep = 2(1+e) = 2(1 + \frac{1}{2}) = 3$, we get $p = 6$.

The polar equation is:
$$
r = \frac{3}{1 + \frac{1}{2}\sin\theta} = \frac{6}{2 + \sin\theta}
$$

content.text: Converting Between Forms

We can convert between polar and rectangular forms using the relationships
$x = r\cos\theta$, $y = r\sin\theta$, and $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$.

content.title: Converting to Rectangular Form
content.solution:
Multiply both sides by $(2 - \sin\theta)$:
$$
r(2 - \sin\theta) = 6
$$
$$
2r - r\sin\theta = 6
$$
Since $r\sin\theta = y$ and $r = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2}$:
$$
2\sqrt{x^2 + y^2} - y = 6
$$
$$
2\sqrt{x^2 + y^2} = y + 6
$$
Squaring both sides:
$$
4(x^2 + y^2) = y^2 + 12y + 36
$$
$$
4x^2 + 4y^2 = y^2 + 12y + 36
$$
$$
4x^2 + 3y^2 - 12y = 36
$$
Completing the square:
$$
4x^2 + 3(y^2 - 4y + 4) = 36 + 12
$$
$$
4x^2 + 3(y-2)^2 = 48
$$
$$
\frac{x^2}{12} + \frac{(y-2)^2}{16} = 1
$$
This is an ellipse with center $(0, 2)$, $a = 4$, $b = 2\sqrt{3}$.

content.text: Kepler's Laws and Orbital Mechanics

One of the most beautiful applications of polar equations of conics is
in describing the orbits of planets, comets, and satellites.

content.title: Kepler's Laws of Planetary Motion
content.content:
1. **Law of Ellipses:** Each planet moves in an elliptical
 orbit with the Sun at one focus.

2. **Law of Equal Areas:** A line segment from the Sun to a
 planet sweeps out equal areas in equal times.

3. **Harmonic Law:** The square of a planet's orbital period
 is proportional to the cube of its semi-major axis: $T^2 \propto a^3$.
content.label: Theorem

content.text:
For an elliptical orbit with semi-major axis $a$ and eccentricity $e$,
the polar equation (with the Sun at the focus) is:
$$
r = \frac{a(1 - e^2)}{1 - e\cos\theta}
$$

The closest approach to the Sun (**perihelion**)
occurs at $\theta = \pi$:
$$
r_{\min} = \frac{a(1-e^2)}{1 - e(-1)} = \frac{a(1-e^2)}{1+e} = a(1-e)
$$

The farthest distance (**aphelion**) occurs at $\theta = 0$:
$$
r_{\max} = \frac{a(1-e^2)}{1 - e} = a(1+e)
$$

content.title: Orbital Distances
content.content:
For an elliptical orbit with semi-major axis $a$ and eccentricity $e$:

- **Perihelion** (closest approach): $r_{\min} = a(1 - e)$

- **Aphelion** (farthest distance): $r_{\max} = a(1 + e)$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Halley's Comet
content.problem:
Halley's comet has an elliptical orbit with eccentricity $e \approx 0.967$
and semi-major axis $a \approx 17.94$ AU (astronomical units, where
1 AU $\approx$ 93 million miles).

**(a)** Find the polar equation of the orbit.

**(b)** Find the perihelion and aphelion distances.
content.solution:
**(a)** The polar equation is:
$$
r = \frac{a(1-e^2)}{1 - e\cos\theta} = \frac{17.94(1 - 0.967^2)}{1 - 0.967\cos\theta}
$$

Calculate $1 - e^2 = 1 - 0.935 = 0.065$:
$$
r = \frac{17.94 \times 0.065}{1 - 0.967\cos\theta} \approx \frac{1.17}{1 - 0.967\cos\theta}
$$

**(b)** Perihelion: $r_{\min} = a(1-e) = 17.94(1 - 0.967) = 17.94 \times 0.033 \approx 0.59$ AU

Aphelion: $r_{\max} = a(1+e) = 17.94(1 + 0.967) = 17.94 \times 1.967 \approx 35.3$ AU

So Halley's comet comes as close as about 55 million miles from the Sun
(inside Venus's orbit!) and travels as far as about 3.3 billion miles
(beyond Neptune's orbit).

figure[H]

Halley's comet orbit (not to scale). The extreme eccentricity
($e = 0.967$) creates a highly elongated ellipse. The comet's perihelion
brings it closer to the Sun than Venus, while its aphelion takes it beyond
Neptune. The small dashed circle represents Earth's orbit for scale.

figure

content.title: Earth's Orbit
content.problem:
Earth's orbit has semi-major axis $a = 1$ AU and eccentricity $e \approx 0.0167$.
Find the polar equation and the variation in Earth's distance from the Sun.
content.solution:
The polar equation is:
$$
r = \frac{1(1 - 0.0167^2)}{1 - 0.0167\cos\theta} \approx \frac{0.99972}{1 - 0.0167\cos\theta}
$$

Perihelion: $r_{\min} = 1(1 - 0.0167) = 0.9833$ AU $\approx$ 91.4 million miles

Aphelion: $r_{\max} = 1(1 + 0.0167) = 1.0167$ AU $\approx$ 94.5 million miles

Earth's distance from the Sun varies by about 3.1 million miles over the
course of a year. Interestingly, Earth is closest to the Sun in early
January (during Northern Hemisphere winter) and farthest in early July!

content.title: Polar Equations of Conics
content.content:
**Standard Forms:** A conic with focus at the pole and directrix at distance $p$:
$$
r = \frac{ep}{1 \pm e\cos\theta} \quad \text{or} \quad r = \frac{ep}{1 \pm e\sin\theta}
$$

**Conic Type by Eccentricity:**

| $0 < e < 1$ | Ellipse (closed curve) |
| $e = 1$ | Parabola (one branch, opens to infinity) |
| $e > 1$ | Hyperbola (two branches) |

**Directrix Orientation:**

- $1 + e\cos\theta$: vertical directrix to the *right* of pole

- $1 - e\cos\theta$: vertical directrix to the *left* of pole

- $1 + e\sin\theta$: horizontal directrix *above* pole

- $1 - e\sin\theta$: horizontal directrix *below* pole

**Orbital Mechanics:** For elliptical orbits with semi-major axis $a$:
$$
r = \frac{a(1-e^2)}{1 - e\cos\theta}, \quad r_{\min} = a(1-e), \quad r_{\max} = a(1+e)
$$

content.problem:
Fill in the blanks.

1. A conic with eccentricity $e < 1$ is an {1in}.

2. A conic with eccentricity $e = 1$ is a {1in}.

3. A conic with eccentricity $e > 1$ is a {1in}.

4. In the polar equation $r = \frac{ep}{1 + e\cos\theta}$, the directrix
 is a {0.5in} line located to the {0.5in}
 of the pole.

5. The closest point of a planet's orbit to the Sun is called the
 {1in}.

True or False. Justify your answer.

1. The polar equations $r = \frac{4}{1+\cos\theta}$ and
 $r = \frac{4}{1-\cos\theta}$ represent the same parabola.

2. Every conic can be written in the form $r = \frac{ep}{1 \pm e\cos\theta}$
 or $r = \frac{ep}{1 \pm e\sin\theta}$.

3. If $e = 0.5$, then the conic is an ellipse.

Mars has an orbital eccentricity of $e \approx 0.0934$ and semi-major axis
$a \approx 1.524$ AU.

1. Find the polar equation of Mars's orbit.

2. Find the perihelion and aphelion distances.

3. How much does Mars's distance from the Sun vary?
content.label: Mars's Orbit

content.problem:
Mercury has the most eccentric orbit of any planet, with $e \approx 0.2056$
and $a \approx 0.387$ AU.

1. Find the polar equation of Mercury's orbit.

2. Find the perihelion and aphelion distances in millions of miles.

3. By what percentage does Mercury's distance from the Sun vary?
content.label: Mercury's Orbit

content.problem:
A comet has a highly eccentric elliptical orbit with perihelion 0.5 AU
and aphelion 50 AU.

1. Find the semi-major axis $a$.

2. Find the eccentricity $e$.

3. Write the polar equation of the orbit.
content.label: Comet's Orbit

content.problem:
A spacecraft leaving Earth follows a hyperbolic trajectory relative to
the Sun with eccentricity $e = 1.3$ and closest approach (perihelion)
of 1 AU.

1. Find the value of $a$ (note: for hyperbolas, $a = r_{\min}/(e-1)$).

2. Write the polar equation of the trajectory.
content.label: Escape Trajectory

content.problem:
Explain why the polar form of a conic with focus at the pole is simpler
than the rectangular form for orbital mechanics applications.

For the equation $r = \frac{ep}{1 - e\cos\theta}$:

1. What happens to the graph as $e$ increases from 0 toward 1?

2. What happens at $e = 1$?

3. What happens as $e$ increases beyond 1?

Explain why a comet in a hyperbolic orbit ($e > 1$) will never return
to the inner solar system.

Prove that for an ellipse with polar equation $r = \frac{a(1-e^2)}{1-e\cos\theta}$:

1. The sum $r_{\min} + r_{\max} = 2a$.

2. The product $r_{\min} \cdot r_{\max} = b^2$, where $b$ is the semi-minor axis.

A satellite is in an elliptical orbit with Earth at one focus. The perigee
(closest approach) is 200 miles above Earth's surface, and the apogee
(farthest point) is 1000 miles above the surface. If Earth's radius is
approximately 4000 miles:

1. Find the semi-major axis of the orbit (measured from Earth's center).

2. Find the eccentricity of the orbit.

3. Write a polar equation for the orbit with Earth's center at the pole.

Kepler's Second Law states that a planet sweeps out equal areas in equal
times. For an ellipse with polar equation $r = \frac{a(1-e^2)}{1-e\cos\theta}$,
the area swept from angle $\theta_1$ to $\theta_2$ is:
$$
A = \frac{1}{2}\int_{\theta_1}^{\theta_2} r^2 \, d\theta
$$
Show that the rate at which area is swept (with respect to time) is
constant if and only if the angular momentum is constant.

Two comets have orbits with the same semi-major axis $a$ but different
eccentricities $e_1 < e_2$. Both orbits have the same focus (the Sun).

1. Which comet comes closer to the Sun?

2. Which comet goes farther from the Sun?

3. Do the orbits have the same period? (Hint: Use Kepler's Third Law.)
